1.en: Can understand with ease virtually any kind of language, whether live or broadcast, delivered at fast natural speed.

2.en: Can understand enough to follow extended discourse on abstract and complex topics beyond their own field, though they may need to confirm occasional details, especially if the variety is unfamiliar.

3.en: Can recognise a wide range of idiomatic expressions and colloquialisms, appreciating register shifts.

4.en: Can follow extended discourse even when it is not clearly structured and when relationships are only implied and not signalled explicitly.

5.en: Can understand standard language or a familiar variety, live or broadcast, on both familiar and unfamiliar topics normally encountered in personal, social, academic or vocational life. Only extreme [auditory/visual] background noise, inadequate discourse structure and/or idiomatic usage influence the ability to understand.

6.en: Can understand the main ideas of propositionally and linguistically complex discourse on both concrete and abstract topics delivered in standard language or a familiar variety, including technical discussions in their field of specialisation.

7.en: Can follow extended discourse and complex lines of argument, provided the topic is reasonably familiar, and the direction of the argument is signposted by explicit markers.

8.en: Can understand straightforward factual information about common everyday or job-related topics, identifying both general messages and specific details, provided people articulate clearly in a generally familiar variety.

9.en: Can understand the main points made in clear standard language or a familiar variety on familiar matters regularly encountered at work, school, leisure, etc., including short narratives.

10.en: Can understand enough to be able to meet needs of a concrete type, provided people articulate clearly and slowly.

11.en: Can understand phrases and expressions related to areas of most immediate priority (e.g. very basic personal and family information, shopping, local geography, employment), provided people articulate clearly and slowly.

12.en: Can follow language which is very slow and carefully articulated, with long pauses for them to assimilate meaning.

13.en: Can recognise concrete information (e.g. places and times) on familiar topics encountered in everyday life, provided it is delivered slowly and clearly.

14.en: Can understand short, very simple questions and statements, provided they are delivered slowly and clearly and accompanied by visuals or manual gestures to support understanding and repeated if necessary.

15.en: Can recognise everyday, familiar words/signs, provided they are delivered clearly and slowly in a clearly defined, familiar everyday context.

16.en: Can recognise numbers, prices, dates and days of the week, provided they are delivered slowly and clearly in a defined, familiar everyday context.

17.en: Can identify the sociocultural implications of most of the language used in colloquial discussions that take place at a natural speed.

18.en: Can easily follow complex interactions between third parties in group discussion and debate, even on abstract, complex, unfamiliar topics.

19.en: Can identify the attitude of each participant in an animated discussion characterised by overlapping turns, digressions and colloquialisms that is delivered at a natural speed in varieties that are familiar.

20.en: Can keep up with an animated conversation between proficient users of the target language.

21.en: Can with some effort catch much of what is said around them, but may find it difficult to participate effectively in discussion with several users of the target language who do not modify their language in any way.

22.en: Can identify the main reasons for and against an argument or idea in a discussion conducted in clear standard language or a familiar variety.

23.en: Can follow chronological sequence in extended informal discourse, e.g. in a story or anecdote.

24.en: Can follow much of everyday conversation and discussion, provided it is clearly articulated in standard language or in a familiar variety.

25.en: Can generally follow the main points of extended discussion around them, provided it is clearly articulated in standard language or a familiar variety.

26.en: Can generally identify the topic of discussion around them when it is conducted slowly and clearly.

27.en: Can recognise when people agree and disagree in a conversation conducted slowly and clearly.

28.en: Can follow in outline short, simple social exchanges, conducted very slowly and clearly.

29.en: Can understand some expressions when people are discussing them, family, school, hobbies or surroundings, provided the delivery is slow and clear.

30.en: Can understand words/signs and short sentences in a simple conversation (e.g. between a customer and a salesperson in a shop), provided people communicate very slowly and very clearly.

32.scale: Understanding as a member of a live audience
32.en: Can follow specialised lectures and presentations employing colloquialism, regional usage or unfamiliar terminology.

33.scale: Understanding as a member of a live audience
33.en: Can make appropriate inferences when links or implications are not made explicit.

34.scale: Understanding as a member of a live audience
34.en: Can get the point of jokes or allusions in a presentation.

35.scale: Understanding as a member of a live audience
35.en: Can follow most lectures, discussions and debates with relative ease.

36.scale: Understanding as a member of a live audience
36.en: Can follow the essentials of lectures, talks and reports and other forms of academic/professional presentation which are propositionally and linguistically complex.

37.scale: Understanding as a member of a live audience
37.en: Can understand the point of view expressed on topics that are of current interest or that relate to their specialised field, provided the talk is delivered in standard language or a familiar variety.

38.scale: Understanding as a member of a live audience
38.en: Can follow complex lines of argument in a clearly articulated lecture, provided the topic is reasonably familiar.

39.scale: Understanding as a member of a live audience
39.en: Can distinguish main themes from asides, provided the lecture or talk is delivered in standard language or a familiar variety.

40.scale: Understanding as a member of a live audience
40.en: Can recognise the point of view expressed and distinguish this from facts being reporting.

41.scale: Understanding as a member of a live audience
41.en: Can follow a lecture or talk within their own field, provided the subject matter is familiar and the presentation straightforward and clearly structured.

42.scale: Understanding as a member of a live audience
42.en: Can distinguish between main ideas and supporting details in standard lectures on familiar subjects, provided these are delivered in clearly articulated standard language or a familiar variety.

43.scale: Understanding as a member of a live audience
43.en: Can follow in outline straightforward short talks on familiar topics, provided these are delivered in clearly articulated standard language or a familiar variety.

44.scale: Understanding as a member of a live audience
44.en: Can follow a straightforward conference presentation or demonstration with visual support (e.g. slides, handouts) on a topic or product within their field, understanding explanations given.

45.scale: Understanding as a member of a live audience
45.en: Can understand the main points of what is said in a straightforward monologue (e.g. a guided tour), provided the delivery is clear and relatively slow.

46.scale: Understanding as a member of a live audience
46.en: Can follow the general outline of a demonstration or presentation on a familiar or predictable topic, where the message is expressed slowly and clearly in simple language and there is visual support (e.g. slides, handouts).

47.scale: Understanding as a member of a live audience
47.en: Can follow a very simple, well-structured presentation or demonstration, provided it is illustrated with slides, concrete examples or diagrams, it is delivered slowly and clearly with repetition, and the topic is familiar.

48.scale: Understanding as a member of a live audience
48.en: Can understand the outline of simple information given in a predictable situation, such as on a guided tour (e.g. “This is where the President lives”).

49.scale: Understanding as a member of a live audience
49.en: Can understand in outline very simple information being explained in a predictable situation like a guided tour, provided the delivery is very slow and clear and that there are long pauses from time to time.

52.en: Can extract specific information from poor quality, [audibly and/or visually] distorted public announcements, e.g. in a station or sports stadium, or on an old recording.

53.en: Can understand complex technical information, such as operating instructions or specifications for familiar products and services.

54.en: Can understand announcements and messages on concrete and abstract topics delivered in standard language or a familiar variety at normal speed.

55.en: Can understand detailed instructions well enough to be able to follow them successfully.

56.en: Can understand simple technical information, such as operating instructions for everyday equipment.

58.en: Can understand public announcements at airports, stations and on planes, buses and trains, provided these are clearly articulated with minimum interference from [auditory/visual] background noise.

59.en: Can understand and follow a series of instructions for familiar everyday activities such as sports, cooking, etc., provided they are delivered slowly and clearly.

60.en: Can understand straightforward announcements (e.g. of a cinema programme or sports event, that a train has been delayed), provided the delivery is slow and clear.

61.en: Can catch the main point in short, clear, simple messages and announcements.

62.en: Can understand simple directions on how to get from X to Y, by foot or public transport.

63.en: Can understand basic instructions on times, dates and numbers, etc., and on routine tasks and assignments to be carried out.

64.en: Can understand instructions addressed carefully and slowly to them and follow short, simple directions.

65.en: Can understand when someone tells them slowly and clearly where something is, provided the object is in the immediate environment.

66.en: Can understand figures, prices and times given slowly and clearly in an announcement by loudspeaker, e.g. at a railway station or in a shop.

67.en: Can understand short, simple instructions for actions such as “Stop”, “Close the door”, etc., provided they are delivered slowly face-to-face, accompanied by pictures or manual gestures and repeated if necessary.

69.scale: Understanding audio (or signed) media and recordings
69.en: Can understand a wide range of recorded and broadcast material, including some non-standard usage, and identify finer points of detail including implicit attitudes and relationships between people.

70.scale: Understanding audio (or signed) media and recordings
70.en: Can understand recordings in the standard form of the language likely to be encountered in social, professional or academic life and identify viewpoints and attitudes as well as the information content.

71.scale: Understanding audio (or signed) media and recordings
71.en: Can understand most documentaries and most other recorded or broadcast material delivered in the standard form of the language and can identify mood, attitude, etc.

72.scale: Understanding audio (or signed) media and recordings
72.en: Can understand the information content of the majority of recorded or broadcast material on topics of personal interest delivered in clear standard language.

73.scale: Understanding audio (or signed) media and recordings
73.en: Can understand the main points of news bulletins and simpler recorded material about familiar subjects delivered relatively slowly and clearly.

74.scale: Understanding audio (or signed) media and recordings
74.en: Can understand the main points and important details in stories and other narratives (e.g. a description of a holiday), provided the delivery is slow and clear.

75.scale: Understanding audio (or signed) media and recordings
75.en: Can understand the most important information contained in short commercials concerning goods and services of interest (e.g. CDs, video games, travel).

76.scale: Understanding audio (or signed) media and recordings
76.en: Can understand in an interview what people say they do in their free time, what they particularly like doing and what they do not like doing, provided they speak slowly and clearly.

77.scale: Understanding audio (or signed) media and recordings
77.en: Can understand and extract the essential information from short, recorded passages dealing with predictable everyday matters which are delivered slowly and clearly.

78.scale: Understanding audio (or signed) media and recordings
78.en: Can extract important information from short broadcasts (e.g. the weather forecast, concert announcements, sports results), provided people talk clearly.

79.scale: Understanding audio (or signed) media and recordings
79.en: Can understand the important points of a story and manage to follow the plot, provided the story is told slowly and clearly.

80.scale: Understanding audio (or signed) media and recordings
80.en: Can pick out concrete information (e.g. places and times) from short recordings on familiar everyday topics, provided they are delivered very slowly and clearly.

81.scale: Understanding audio (or signed) media and recordings
81.en: Can recognise words/signs, names and numbers that they already know in simple, short recordings, provided these are delivered very slowly and clearly.

83.en: Can follow films employing a considerable degree of slang and idiomatic usage.

84.en: Can understand in detail the arguments presented in demanding television broadcasts such as current affairs programmes, interviews, discussion programmes and chat shows.

85.en: Can understand nuances and implied meaning in most films, plays and TV programmes, provided these are delivered in standard language or a familiar variety.

86.en: Can extract the main points from the arguments and discussions in news and current affairs programmes.

87.en: Can understand most TV news and current affairs programmes.

88.en: Can understand documentaries, live interviews, talk shows, plays and the majority of films in the standard form of the language or a familiar variety.

89.en: Can understand a large part of many TV programmes on topics of personal interest such as interviews, short lectures and news reports when the delivery is relatively slow and clear.

90.en: Can follow many films in which visuals and action carry much of the storyline, and which are delivered clearly in straightforward language.

91.en: Can catch the main points in TV programmes on familiar topics when the delivery is relatively slow and clear.

92.en: Can identify the main point of TV news items reporting events, accidents, etc. where the visuals support the commentary.

93.en: Can follow a TV commercial or a trailer for or scene from a film, understanding what topic(s) are concerned, provided the images are a great help in understanding and the delivery is clear and relatively slow.

94.en: Can follow changes of topic of factual TV news items, and form an idea of the main content.

95.en: Can recognise familiar words/signs and phrases and identify the topics in headline news summaries and many of the products in advertisements, by exploiting visual information and general knowledge.

96.en: Can identify the subject of a video document on the basis of visual information and previous knowledge.

97.en: Can understand virtually all types of texts including abstract, structurally complex, or highly colloquial literary and non-literary writings.

98.en: Can understand a wide range of long and complex texts, appreciating subtle distinctions of style and implicit as well as explicit meaning.

99.en: Can understand in detail lengthy, complex texts, whether or not these relate to their own area of speciality, provided they can reread difficult sections.

100.en: Can understand a wide variety of texts including literary writings, newspaper or magazine articles, and specialised academic or professional publications, provided there are opportunities for rereading and they have access to reference tools.

101.en: Can read with a large degree of independence, adapting style and speed of reading to different texts and purposes, and using appropriate reference sources selectively. Has a broad active reading vocabulary, but may experience some difficulty with low-frequency idioms.

102.en: Can read straightforward factual texts on subjects related to their field of interest with a satisfactory level of comprehension.

103.en: Can understand short, simple texts on familiar matters of a concrete type which consist of high frequency everyday or job-related language.

104.en: Can understand short, simple texts containing the highest frequency vocabulary, including a proportion of shared international vocabulary items.

105.en: Can understand very short, simple texts a single phrase at a time, picking up familiar names, words and basic phrases and rereading as required.

106.en: Can recognise familiar words/signs accompanied by pictures, such as a fast-food restaurant menu illustrated with photos or a picture book using familiar vocabulary.

107.en: Can understand specialised, formal correspondence on a complex topic.

108.en: Can understand any correspondence given the occasional use of a dictionary.

109.en: Can understand implicit as well as explicit attitudes, emotions and opinions expressed in e-mails, discussion forums, vlogs/blogs, etc., provided there are opportunities for rereading and they have access to reference tools.

110.en: Can understand slang, idiomatic expressions and jokes in private correspondence.

111.en: Can read correspondence relating to their field of interest and readily grasp the essential meaning.

112.en: Can understand what is said in a personal e-mail or posting even where some colloquial language is used.

113.en: Can understand formal correspondence on less familiar subjects well enough to redirect it to someone else.

114.en: Can understand the description of events, feelings and wishes in personal letters well enough to correspond regularly with a pen friend.

115.en: Can understand straightforward personal letters, e-mails or postings giving a relatively detailed account of events and experiences.

116.en: Can understand standard formal correspondence and online postings in their area of professional interest.

117.en: Can understand a simple personal letter, e-mail or post in which the person writing is talking about familiar subjects (such as friends or family) or asking questions on these subjects.

118.en: Can understand basic types of standard routine letters and faxes (enquiries, orders, letters of confirmation, etc.) on familiar topics.

119.en: Can understand short, simple personal letters.

120.en: Can understand very simple formal e-mails and letters (e.g. confirmation of a booking or online purchase).

121.en: Can understand short, simple messages on postcards.

122.en: Can understand short, simple messages sent via social media or e-mail (e.g. proposing what to do, when and where to meet).

123.en: Can understand from a letter, card or e-mail the event to which they are being invited and the information given about day, time and location.

124.en: Can recognise times and places in very simple notes and text messages from friends or colleagues (e.g. “Back at 4 o’clock” or “In the meeting room”), provided there are no abbreviations.

127.en: Can scan quickly through several sources (articles, reports, websites, books, etc.) in parallel, in both their own field and in related fields, and can identify the relevance and usefulness of particular sections for the task at hand.

128.en: Can scan quickly through long and complex texts, locating relevant details.

129.en: Can quickly identify the content and relevance of news items, articles and reports on a wide range of professional topics, deciding whether closer study is worthwhile.

130.en: Can scan longer texts in order to locate desired information, and gather information from different parts of a text, or from different texts in order to fulfil a specific task.

131.en: Can scan through straightforward, factual texts in magazines, brochures or on the web, identify what they are about and decide whether they contain information that might be of practical use.

132.en: Can find and understand relevant information in everyday material, such as letters, brochures and short official documents.

133.en: Can pick out important information about preparation and usage on the labels on foodstuff and medicine.

134.en: Can assess whether an article, report or review is on the required topic.

135.en: Can understand the important information in simple, clearly drafted adverts in newspapers or magazines, provided there are not too many abbreviations.

136.en: Can find specific information in practical, concrete, predictable texts (e.g. travel guidebooks, recipes), provided they are produced in simple language.

137.en: Can understand the main information in short and simple descriptions of goods in brochures and websites (e.g. portable digital devices, cameras).

138.en: Can find specific, predictable information in simple everyday material such as advertisements, prospectuses, menus, reference lists and timetables.

139.en: Can locate specific information in lists and isolate the information required (e.g. use the Yellow Pages to find a service or tradesman).

140.en: Can understand everyday signs and notices, etc. in public places, such as streets, restaurants, railway stations; in workplaces, such as directions, instructions, hazard warnings.

141.en: Can recognise familiar names, words/signs and very basic phrases on simple notices in the most common everyday situations.

142.en: Can understand store guides (information on which floors departments are on) and directions (e.g. where to find lifts).

143.en: Can understand basic hotel information (e.g. times when meals are served).

144.en: Can find and understand simple, important information in advertisements, programmes for special events, leaflets and brochures (e.g. what is proposed, costs, the date and place of the event, departure times).

145.en: Can understand simple everyday signs such as “Parking”, “Station”, “Dining room”, “No smoking”, etc.

146.en: Can find information about places, times and prices on posters, flyers and notices.

147.en: Can understand the finer points and implications of a complex report or article even outside their area of specialisation.

148.en: Can understand in detail a wide range of lengthy, complex texts likely to be encountered in social, professional or academic life, identifying finer points of detail including attitudes and implied as well as stated opinions.

149.en: Can obtain information, ideas and opinions from highly specialised sources within their field.

150.en: Can understand specialised articles outside their field, provided they can use a dictionary occasionally to confirm their interpretation of terminology.

151.en: Can understand articles and reports concerned with contemporary problems in which particular stances or viewpoints are adopted.

152.en: Can recognise when a text provides factual information and when it seeks to convince readers of something.

153.en: Can recognise different structures in discursive text: contrasting arguments, problem–solution presentation and cause–effect relationships.

154.en: Can understand straightforward, factual texts on subjects relating to their interests or studies.

155.en: Can understand short texts on subjects that are familiar or of current interest, in which people give their points of view (e.g. critical contributions to an online discussion forum or readers’ letters to the editor).

156.en: Can identify the main conclusions in clearly signalled argumentative texts.

157.en: Can recognise the line of argument in the treatment of the issue presented, though not necessarily in detail.

158.en: Can recognise significant points in straightforward news articles on familiar subjects.

159.en: Can understand most factual information that they are likely to come across on familiar subjects of interest, provided they have sufficient time for rereading.

160.en: Can understand the main points in descriptive notes such as those on museum exhibits and explanatory boards in exhibitions.

161.en: Can identify specific information in simpler material they encounter such as letters, brochures and short news articles describing events.

162.en: Can follow the general outline of a news report on a familiar type of event, provided the contents are familiar and predictable.

163.en: Can pick out the main information in short news reports or simple articles in which figures, names, illustrations and titles play a prominent role and support the meaning of the text.

164.en: Can understand the main points of short texts dealing with everyday topics (e.g. lifestyle, hobbies, sports, weather).

165.en: Can understand texts describing people, places, everyday life and culture, etc., provided they use simple language.

166.en: Can understand information given in illustrated brochures and maps (e.g. the principal attractions of a city).

167.en: Can understand the main points in short news items on subjects of personal interest (e.g. sport, celebrities).

168.en: Can understand a short factual description or report within their own field, provided simple language is used and that it does not contain unpredictable detail.

169.en: Can understand most of what people say about themselves in a personal ad or post and what they say they like in other people.

170.en: Can get an idea of the content of simpler informational material and short, simple descriptions, especially if there is visual support.

171.en: Can understand short texts on subjects of personal interest (e.g. news flashes about sports, music, travel or stories) composed in very simple language and supported by illustrations and pictures.

172.en: Can understand the simplest informational material such as a fast-food restaurant menu illustrated with photos or an illustrated story formulated in very simple everyday words/signs.

174.en: Can understand in detail lengthy, complex instructions on a new machine or procedure, whether or not the instructions relate to their own area of speciality, provided they can reread difficult sections.

175.en: Can understand lengthy, complex instructions in their field, including details on conditions and warnings, provided they can reread difficult sections.

176.en: Can understand instructions and procedures in the form of a continuous text, for instance in a manual, provided they are familiar with the type of process or product concerned.

177.en: Can understand clearly expressed, straightforward instructions for a piece of equipment.

178.en: Can follow simple instructions given on packaging (e.g. cooking instructions).

179.en: Can understand most short safety instructions, (e.g. on public transport or in manuals for the use of electrical equipment).

180.en: Can understand regulations, for example safety, when expressed in simple language.

181.en: Can understand short instructions illustrated step by step (e.g. for installing new technology).

182.en: Can understand simple instructions on equipment encountered in everyday life – such as a public telephone.

183.en: Can understand simple, brief instructions, provided they are illustrated and not presented in continuous text.

184.en: Can understand instructions on medicine labels expressed as a simple command (e.g. “Take before meals” or “Do not take if driving”).

185.en: Can follow a simple recipe, especially if there are pictures to illustrate the most important steps.

186.en: Can follow short, simple directions (e.g. to go from X to Y).

187.en: Can understand very short, simple, instructions used in familiar everyday contexts (e.g. “No parking”, “No food or drink”), especially if there are illustrations.

188.en: Can read virtually all forms of texts including classical or colloquial literary and non-literary texts in different genres, appreciating subtle distinctions of style and implicit as well as explicit meaning.

189.en: Can read and appreciate a variety of literary texts, provided they can reread certain sections and that they can access reference tools if they wish.

190.en: Can read contemporary literary texts and non-fiction produced in the standard form of the language or a familiar variety with little difficulty and with appreciation of implicit meanings and ideas.

191.en: Can read for pleasure with a large degree of independence, adapting style and speed of reading to different texts (e.g. magazines, more straightforward novels, history books, biographies, travelogues, guides, lyrics, poems), using appropriate reference sources selectively.

192.en: Can read novels with a strong, narrative plot and that use straightforward, unelaborated language, provided they can take their time and use a dictionary.

193.en: Can read newspaper/magazine accounts of films, books, concerts, etc. produced for a wider audience and understand the main points.

194.en: Can understand simple poems and song lyrics provided these employ straightforward language and style.

195.en: Can understand descriptions of places, events, explicitly expressed feelings and perspectives in narratives, guides and magazine articles that employ high frequency everyday language.

196.en: Can understand a travel diary mainly describing the events of a journey and the experiences and discoveries of the writer.

197.en: Can follow the plot of stories, simple novels and comics with a clear linear storyline and high frequency everyday language, given regular use of a dictionary.

198.en: Can understand enough to read short, simple stories and comic strips involving familiar, concrete situations described in high frequency everyday language.

199.en: Can understand the main points made in short magazine reports or guide entries that deal with concrete everyday topics (e.g. hobbies, sports, leisure activities, animals).

200.en: Can understand short narratives and descriptions of someone’s life composed in simple language.

201.en: Can understand what is happening in a photo story (e.g. in a lifestyle magazine) and form an impression of what the characters are like.

202.en: Can understand much of the information provided in a short description of a person (e.g. a celebrity).

203.en: Can understand the main point of a short article reporting an event that follows a predictable pattern (e.g. the Oscars), provided it is composed clearly in simple language.

204.en: Can understand short, illustrated narratives about everyday activities described in simple words.

205.en: Can understand in outline short texts in illustrated stories, provided the images help them to guess at a lot of the content.

208.scale: Identifying cues and inferring (spoken, signed and written)
208.en: Is skilled at using contextual, grammatical and lexical cues to infer attitude, mood and intentions and anticipate what will come next.

209.scale: Identifying cues and inferring (spoken, signed and written)
209.en: Can use a variety of strategies to achieve comprehension, including watching out for main points and checking comprehension by using contextual clues.

210.scale: Identifying cues and inferring (spoken, signed and written)
210.en: Can exploit different types of connectors (numerical, temporal, logical) and the role of key paragraphs in the overall organisation in order to better understand the argumentation in a text.

211.scale: Identifying cues and inferring (spoken, signed and written)
211.en: Can extrapolate the meaning of a section of a text by taking into account the text as a whole.

212.scale: Identifying cues and inferring (spoken, signed and written)
212.en: Can identify the meaning of unfamiliar words/signs from the context on topics related to their field and interests.

213.scale: Identifying cues and inferring (spoken, signed and written)
213.en: Can extrapolate the meaning of occasional unknown words/signs from the context and deduce sentence meaning, provided the topic discussed is familiar.

214.scale: Identifying cues and inferring (spoken, signed and written)
214.en: Can make basic inferences or predictions about text content from headings, titles or headlines.

215.scale: Identifying cues and inferring (spoken, signed and written)
215.en: Can watch or listen to a short narrative and predict what will happen next.

216.scale: Identifying cues and inferring (spoken, signed and written)
216.en: Can follow a line of argumentation or the sequence of events in a story, by focusing on common logical connectors (e.g. however, because) and temporal connectors (e.g. after that, beforehand).

217.scale: Identifying cues and inferring (spoken, signed and written)
217.en: Can deduce the probable meaning of unknown words/signs in a text by identifying their constituent parts (e.g. identifying roots, lexical elements, suffixes and prefixes).

218.scale: Identifying cues and inferring (spoken, signed and written)
218.en: Can use an idea of the overall meaning of short texts and utterances on everyday topics of a concrete type to derive the probable meaning of unknown words/signs from the context.

219.scale: Identifying cues and inferring (spoken, signed and written)
219.en: Can exploit their recognition of known words/signs to deduce the meaning of unfamiliar words/signs in short expressions used in routine everyday contexts.

220.scale: Identifying cues and inferring (spoken, signed and written)
220.en: Can exploit format, appearance and typographic features in order to identify the type of text: news story, promotional text, article, textbook, chat or forum, etc.

221.scale: Identifying cues and inferring (spoken, signed and written)
221.en: Can exploit numbers, dates, names, proper nouns, etc. to identify the topic of a text.

222.scale: Identifying cues and inferring (spoken, signed and written)
222.en: Can deduce the meaning and function of unknown formulaic expressions from their position in a text (e.g. at the beginning or end of a letter).

223.scale: Identifying cues and inferring (spoken, signed and written)
223.en: Can deduce the meaning of an unknown word/sign for a concrete action or object, provided the surrounding text is very simple, and on a familiar everyday subject.

224.scale: Identifying cues and inferring (spoken, signed and written)
224.en: Can guess the probable meaning of an unknown word/sign that is similar to one in the language they normally use.

225.scale: Identifying cues and inferring (spoken, signed and written)
225.en: Can deduce the meaning of a word/sign from an accompanying picture or icon.

226.en: Can produce clear, smoothly flowing, well-structured discourse with an effective logical structure which helps the recipient to notice and remember significant points.

227.en: Can give clear, detailed descriptions and presentations on complex subjects, integrating sub-themes, developing particular points and rounding off with an appropriate conclusion.

228.en: Can give clear, systematically developed descriptions and presentations, with appropriate highlighting of significant points, and relevant supporting detail.

229.en: Can give clear, detailed descriptions and presentations on a wide range of subjects related to their field of interest, expanding and supporting ideas with subsidiary points and relevant examples.

230.en: Can reasonably fluently sustain a straightforward description of one of a variety of subjects within their field of interest, presenting it as a linear sequence of points.

231.en: Can give a simple description or presentation of people, living or working conditions, daily routines. likes/dislikes, etc. as a short series of simple phrases and sentences linked into a list.

232.en: Can produce simple, mainly isolated phrases about people and places.

233.en: Can produce short phrases about themselves, giving basic personal information (e.g. name, address, family, nationality).

234.en: Can give clear, smoothly flowing, elaborate and often memorable descriptions.

235.en: Can give clear, detailed descriptions of complex subjects.

236.en: Can give elaborate descriptions and narratives, integrating sub-themes, developing particular points and rounding them off with an appropriate conclusion.

237.en: Can give clear, detailed descriptions on a wide range of subjects related to their field of interest.

238.en: Can describe the personal significance of events and experiences in detail.

239.en: Can clearly express feelings about something experienced and give reasons to explain those feelings.

240.en: Can give straightforward descriptions on a variety of familiar subjects within their field of interest.

241.en: Can reasonably fluently relate a straightforward narrative or description as a sequence of points.

242.en: Can give detailed accounts of experiences, describing feelings and reactions.

243.en: Can relate details of unpredictable occurrences, e.g. an accident.

244.en: Can relate the plot of a book or film and describe their reactions.

245.en: Can describe dreams, hopes and ambitions.

246.en: Can describe events, real or imagined.

248.en: Can tell a story or describe something in a simple list of points.

249.en: Can describe everyday aspects of their environment e.g. people, places, a job or study experience.

250.en: Can give short, basic descriptions of events and activities.

251.en: Can describe plans and arrangements, habits and routines, past activities and personal experiences.

252.en: Can use simple descriptive language to make brief statements about and compare objects and possessions.

253.en: Can explain what they like or dislike about something.

254.en: Can describe their family, living conditions, educational background, present or most recent job.

255.en: Can describe people, places and possessions in simple terms.

256.en: Can express what they are good at and not so good at (e.g. sports, games, skills, subjects).

257.en: Can briefly describe what they plan to do at the weekend or during the holidays.

258.en: Can describe themselves, what they do and where they live.

259.en: Can describe simple aspects of their everyday life in a series of simple sentences, using simple words/signs and basic phrases, provided they can prepare in advance.

260.en: Can describe themselves (e.g. name, age, family), using simple words/signs and formulaic expressions, provided they can prepare in advance.

261.en: Can express how they are feeling using simple adjectives like “happy” or “tired”, accompanied by body language.

263.en: Can communicate clearly detailed distinctions between ideas, concepts and things that closely resemble one other.

264.en: Can give instructions on carrying out a series of complex professional or academic procedures.

265.en: Can communicate complex information and advice on the full range of matters related to their occupational role.

267.en: Can give a clear, detailed description of how to carry out a procedure.

268.en: Can explain the main points in an idea or problem with reasonable precision.

269.en: Can describe how to do something, giving detailed instructions.

270.en: Can report straightforward factual information on a familiar topic, for example to indicate the nature of a problem or to give detailed directions, provided they can prepare beforehand.

271.en: Can give simple directions on how to get from X to Y, using basic expressions such as “turn right” and “go straight” along with sequential connectors such as “first”, “then” and “next”.

272.en: Can name an object and indicate its shape and colour while showing it to others using basic words/signs, phrases and formulaic expressions, provided they can prepare in advance.

275.scale: Sustained monologue: putting a case (e.g. in a debate)
275.en: Can argue a case on a complex issue, formulating points precisely and employing emphasis effectively.

276.scale: Sustained monologue: putting a case (e.g. in a debate)
276.en: Can develop an argument systematically in well-structured language, taking into account the interlocutor’s perspective, highlighting significant points with supporting examples and concluding appropriately.

277.scale: Sustained monologue: putting a case (e.g. in a debate)
277.en: Can develop an argument systematically with appropriate highlighting of significant points, and relevant supporting detail.

278.scale: Sustained monologue: putting a case (e.g. in a debate)
278.en: Can develop a clear argument, expanding and supporting their points of view at some length with subsidiary points and relevant examples.

279.scale: Sustained monologue: putting a case (e.g. in a debate)
279.en: Can construct a chain of reasoned argument.

280.scale: Sustained monologue: putting a case (e.g. in a debate)
280.en: Can explain a viewpoint on a topical issue giving the advantages and disadvantages of various options.

281.scale: Sustained monologue: putting a case (e.g. in a debate)
281.en: Can develop an argument well enough to be followed without difficulty most of the time.

282.scale: Sustained monologue: putting a case (e.g. in a debate)
282.en: Can give simple reasons to justify a viewpoint on a familiar topic.

283.scale: Sustained monologue: putting a case (e.g. in a debate)
283.en: Can express opinions on subjects relating to everyday life, using simple expressions.

284.scale: Sustained monologue: putting a case (e.g. in a debate)
284.en: Can briefly give reasons and explanations for opinions, plans and actions.

285.scale: Sustained monologue: putting a case (e.g. in a debate)
285.en: Can explain whether or not they approve of what someone has done and give reasons to justify this opinion.

286.scale: Sustained monologue: putting a case (e.g. in a debate)
286.en: Can explain what they like or dislike about something, why they prefer one thing to another, making simple, direct comparisons.

287.scale: Sustained monologue: putting a case (e.g. in a debate)
287.en: Can present their opinion in simple terms, provided interlocutors are patient.

291.en: Can deliver announcements fluently, almost effortlessly, using stress and intonation to convey finer shades of meaning precisely.

292.en: Can deliver announcements on most general topics with a degree of clarity, fluency and spontaneity which causes no strain or inconvenience to the recipient.

293.en: Can deliver short, rehearsed announcements on a topic pertinent to everyday occurrences in their field which, despite possible problems with stress and intonation (= non-manuals in a sign language), are nevertheless clearly intelligible.

294.en: Can deliver very short, rehearsed announcements of predictable, learnt content which are intelligible to recipients who are prepared to concentrate.

297.en: Can present a complex topic confidently and articulately to an audience unfamiliar with it, structuring and adapting the talk flexibly to meet the audience’s needs.

298.en: Can handle difficult and even hostile questioning.

299.en: Can give a clear, well-structured presentation on a complex subject, expanding and supporting points of view at some length with subsidiary points, reasons and relevant examples.

300.en: Can structure a longer presentation appropriately in order to help the audience follow the sequence of ideas and understand the overall argumentation.

301.en: Can speculate or hypothesise in presenting a complex subject, comparing and evaluating alternative proposals and arguments.

302.en: Can handle interjections well, responding spontaneously and almost effortlessly.

303.en: Can give a clear, systematically developed presentation, with highlighting of significant points, and relevant supporting detail.

304.en: Can depart spontaneously from a prepared text and follow up interesting points raised by members of the audience, often showing remarkable fluency and ease of expression.

305.en: Can give a clear, prepared presentation, giving reasons in support of or against a particular point of view and giving the advantages and disadvantages of various options.

306.en: Can take a series of follow-up questions with a degree of fluency and spontaneity which poses no strain for either themselves or the audience.

307.en: Can give a prepared presentation on a familiar topic within their field, outlining similarities and differences (e.g. between products, countries/regions, plans).

308.en: Can give a prepared straightforward presentation on a familiar topic within their field which is clear enough to be followed without difficulty most of the time, and in which the main points are explained with reasonable precision.

309.en: Can take follow-up questions, but may have to ask for repetition if the delivery is rapid.

310.en: Can give a short, rehearsed presentation on a topic pertinent to their everyday life, and briefly give reasons and explanations for opinions, plans and actions.

311.en: Can cope with a limited number of straightforward follow-up questions.

312.en: Can give a short, rehearsed, basic presentation on a familiar subject.

313.en: Can answer straightforward follow-up questions if they can ask for repetition and if some help with the formulation of their reply is possible.

314.en: Can use a very short prepared text to deliver a rehearsed statement (e.g. to formally introduce someone, to propose a toast).

316.en: Can produce clear, smoothly flowing, complex texts in an appropriate and effective style and a logical structure which helps the reader identify significant points.

317.en: Can produce clear, well-structured texts of complex subjects, underlining the relevant salient issues, expanding and supporting points of view at some length with subsidiary points, reasons and relevant examples, and rounding off with an appropriate conclusion.

318.en: Can employ the structure and conventions of a variety of genres, varying the tone, style and register according to addressee, text type and theme.

319.en: Can produce clear, detailed texts on a variety of subjects related to their field of interest, synthesising and evaluating information and arguments from a number of sources.

320.en: Can produce straightforward connected texts on a range of familiar subjects within their field of interest, by linking a series of shorter discrete elements into a linear sequence.

321.en: Can produce a series of simple phrases and sentences linked with simple connectors like “and”, “but” and “because”.

322.en: Can give information about matters of personal relevance (e.g. likes and dislikes, family, pets) using simple words/signs and basic expressions.

323.en: Can produce simple isolated phrases and sentences.

324.en: Can give basic personal information (e.g. name, address, nationality), perhaps with the use of a dictionary.

325.en: Can relate clear, smoothly flowing and engaging stories and descriptions of experience in a style appropriate to the genre adopted.

326.en: Can exploit idiom and humour appropriately to enhance the impact of the text.

327.en: Can produce clear, detailed, well-structured and developed descriptions and imaginative texts in an assured, personal, natural style appropriate to the reader in mind.

328.en: Can incorporate idiom and humour, though use of the latter is not always appropriate.

329.en: Can give a detailed critical review of cultural events (e.g. plays, films, concerts) or literary works.

330.en: Can give clear, detailed descriptions of real or imaginary events and experiences marking the relationship between ideas in clear connected text, and following established conventions of the genre concerned.

331.en: Can give clear, detailed descriptions on a variety of subjects related to their field of interest.

332.en: Can give a review of a film, book or play.

333.en: Can clearly signal chronological sequence in narrative text.

334.en: Can give a simple review of a film, book or TV programme using a limited range of language.

335.en: Can give straightforward, detailed descriptions on a range of familiar subjects within their field of interest.

336.en: Can give accounts of experiences, describing feelings and reactions in simple, connected text.

337.en: Can give a description of an event, a recent trip – real or imagined.

339.en: Can describe everyday aspects of their environment e.g. people, places, a job or study experience in linked sentences.

340.en: Can give very short, basic descriptions of events, past activities and personal experiences.

341.en: Can tell a simple story (e.g. about events on a holiday or about life in the distant future).

342.en: Can produce a series of simple phrases and sentences about their family, living conditions, educational background, or present or most recent job.

343.en: Can create short, simple imaginary biographies and simple poems about people.

344.en: Can create diary entries that describe activities (e.g. daily routine, outings, sports, hobbies), people and places, using basic, concrete vocabulary and simple phrases and sentences with simple connectives like “and”, “but” and “because”.

345.en: Can compose an introduction to a story or continue a story, provided they can consult a dictionary and references (e.g. tables of verb tenses in a course book).

346.en: Can produce simple phrases and sentences about themselves and imaginary people, where they live and what they do.

347.en: Can describe in very simple language what a room looks like.

348.en: Can use simple words/signs and phrases to describe certain everyday objects (e.g. the colour of a car, whether it is big or small).

350.en: Can produce clear, smoothly flowing, complex reports, articles or essays which present a case, or give critical appreciation of proposals or literary works.

351.en: Can provide an appropriate and effective logical structure which helps the reader identify significant points.

352.en: Can set out multiple perspectives on complex academic or professional topics, clearly distinguishing their own ideas and opinions from those in the sources.

353.en: Can produce clear, well-structured expositions of complex subjects, underlining the relevant salient issues.

354.en: Can expand and support points of view at some length with subsidiary points, reasons and relevant examples.

355.en: Can produce a suitable introduction and conclusion to a longer report, article or dissertation on a complex academic or professional topic provided the topic is within their field of interest and there are opportunities for redrafting and revision.

356.en: Can produce an essay or report which develops an argument systematically with appropriate highlighting of significant points and relevant supporting detail.

357.en: Can produce a detailed description of a complex process.

358.en: Can evaluate different ideas or solutions to a problem.

359.en: Can produce an essay or report which develops an argument, giving reasons in support of or against a particular point of view and explaining the advantages and disadvantages of various options.

360.en: Can synthesise information and arguments from a number of sources.

361.en: Can produce short, simple essays on topics of interest.

362.en: Can produce a text on a topical subject of personal interest, using simple language to list advantages and disadvantages, and give and justify their opinion.

363.en: Can summarise, report and give their opinion about accumulated factual information on familiar routine and non-routine matters within their field with some confidence.

364.en: Can produce very brief reports in a standard conventionalised format, which pass on routine factual information and state reasons for actions.

365.en: Can present a topic in a short report or poster, using photographs and short blocks of text.

366.en: Can produce simple texts on familiar subjects of interest, linking sentences with connectors like “and”, “because” or “then”.

367.en: Can give their impressions and opinions about topics of personal interest (e.g. lifestyles and culture, stories), using basic everyday vocabulary and expressions.

371.en: Can, when preparing a more formal text, consciously adopt the conventions linked to the particular type of text concerned (e.g. structure, level of formality).

372.en: Can, in preparing for a potentially complicated or awkward situation, plan what to say in the event of different reactions, reflecting on what expression would be appropriate.

373.en: Can plan what is to be said and the means to say it, considering the effect on the recipient(s).

374.en: Can rehearse and try out new combinations and expressions, inviting feedback.

375.en: Can work out how to communicate the main point(s) they want to get across, exploiting any resources available and limiting the message to what they can recall or find the means to express.

376.en: Can recall and rehearse an appropriate set of phrases from their repertoire.

379.en: Can substitute an equivalent term for a word/sign they can’t recall, so smoothly that it is scarcely noticeable.

380.en: Can exploit their range of vocabulary options creatively so as to readily and effectively use circumlocution in almost all situations.

381.en: Can use circumlocution and paraphrase to cover gaps in vocabulary and structure.

382.en: Can address most communication problems by using circumlocution, or by avoiding difficult expressions.

383.en: Can define the features of something concrete for which they can’t remember the word/sign.

384.en: Can convey meaning by qualifying a word/sign meaning something similar (e.g. a truck for people = bus).

385.en: Can use a simple word/sign meaning something similar to the concept they want to convey and invite “correction”.

386.en: Can “foreignise” word/signs in their first language and ask for confirmation.

387.en: Can use an inadequate word/sign from their repertoire and use gestures to clarify what they mean.

388.en: Can identify what they mean by pointing to it (e.g. “I’d like this, please”).

389.en: Can use gestures to support simple words/signs in expressing a need.

390.en: Can point to something and ask what it is.

391.en: Can backtrack and restructure around a difficulty so smoothly that the interlocutor is hardly aware of it.

392.en: Can backtrack when they encounter a difficulty and reformulate what they want to say without fully interrupting the flow of language.

393.en: Can self-correct with a high degree of effectiveness.

394.en: Can often retrospectively self-correct their occasional “slips” or non-systematic errors and minor flaws in sentence structure.

395.en: Can correct slips and errors that they become conscious of, or that have led to misunderstandings.

396.en: Can make a note of their recurring mistakes and consciously monitor for them.

397.en: Can correct mix-ups with the marking of time or expressions that lead to misunderstandings, provided the interlocutor indicates there is a problem.

398.en: Can ask for confirmation that a form used is correct.

399.en: Can start again using a different tactic when communication breaks down.

403.en: Has a good command of idiomatic expressions and colloquialisms with awareness of connotative levels of meaning. Can convey finer shades of meaning precisely by using, with reasonable accuracy, a wide range of modification devices. Can backtrack and restructure around a difficulty so smoothly that the interlocutor is hardly aware of it.

404.en: Can express themselves fluently and spontaneously, almost effortlessly. Has a good command of a broad lexical repertoire allowing gaps to be readily overcome with circumlocutions. There is little obvious searching for expressions or avoidance strategies; only a conceptually difficult subject can hinder a natural, smooth flow of language.

405.en: Can use the language fluently, accurately and effectively on a wide range of general, academic, vocational or leisure topics, marking clearly the relationships between ideas. Can communicate spontaneously with good grammatical control without much sign of having to restrict what they want to say, adopting a level of formality appropriate to the circumstances.

406.en: Can interact with a degree of fluency and spontaneity that makes regular interaction, and sustained relationships with users of the target language, quite possible without imposing strain on either party. Can highlight the personal significance of events and experiences, and account for and sustain views clearly by providing relevant explanations and arguments.

407.en: Can communicate with some confidence on familiar routine and non-routine matters related to their interests and professional field. Can exchange, check and confirm information, deal with less routine situations and explain why something is a problem. Can express thoughts on more abstract, cultural topics such as films, books, music, etc.

408.en: Can exploit a wide range of simple language to deal with most situations likely to arise whilst travelling. Can enter unprepared into conversation on familiar topics, and express personal opinions and exchange information on topics that are familiar, of personal interest or pertinent to everyday life (e.g. family, hobbies, work, travel and current events).

409.en: Can interact with reasonable ease in structured situations and short conversations, provided the other person helps if necessary. Can manage simple, routine exchanges without undue effort; can ask and answer questions and exchange ideas and information on familiar topics in predictable everyday situations.

410.en: Can communicate in simple and routine tasks requiring a simple and direct exchange of information on familiar and routine matters to do with work and free time. Can handle very short social exchanges but is rarely able to understand enough to keep conversation going of their own accord.

411.en: Can interact in a simple way but communication is totally dependent on repetition at a slower rate, rephrasing and repair. Can ask and answer simple questions, initiate and respond to simple statements in areas of immediate need or on very familiar topics.

412.en: Can ask and answer questions about themselves and daily routines, using short, formulaic expressions and relying on gestures to reinforce the information.

413.en: Can understand any interlocutor, even on abstract and complex topics of a specialist nature beyond their own field, given an opportunity to adjust to a less familiar variety.

414.en: Can understand an interlocutor in detail on abstract and complex topics of a specialist nature beyond their own field, though they may need to confirm occasional details, especially if the variety is unfamiliar.

415.en: Can understand in detail what is said to them in the standard language or a familiar variety even in a [audially/visually] noisy environment.

416.en: Can follow clearly articulated speech/sign directed at them in everyday conversation, though will sometimes have to ask for repetition of particular words/signs and phrases.

417.en: Can understand enough to manage simple, routine exchanges without undue effort.

418.en: Can generally understand clear, standard speech/sign on familiar matters directed at them, provided they can ask for repetition or reformulation from time to time.

419.en: Can understand what is said clearly, slowly and directly to them in simple everyday conversation; can be made to understand, if the interlocutor can take the trouble.

420.en: Can understand everyday expressions aimed at the satisfaction of simple needs of a concrete type, delivered directly to them clearly and slowly, with repetition, by a sympathetic interlocutor.

421.en: Can understand questions and instructions addressed carefully and slowly to them and follow short, simple directions.

422.en: Can understand simple questions that directly concern them (e.g. name, age and address), if the person is asking slowly and clearly.

423.en: Can understand simple personal information (e.g. name, age, place of residence, origin) when other people introduce themselves slowly and clearly, directly to them, and can understand questions on this theme addressed to them, though the questions may need to be repeated.

424.en: Can understand a number of familiar words/signs and recognise key information (e.g. numbers, prices, dates and days of the week), provided the delivery is very slow, with repetition if necessary.

425.en: Can converse comfortably and appropriately, unhampered by any linguistic limitations in conducting a full social and personal life.

426.en: Can use language flexibly and effectively for social purposes, including emotional, allusive and joking usage.

427.en: Can establish a relationship with interlocutors through sympathetic questioning and expressions of agreement plus, if appropriate, comments about third parties or shared conditions.

428.en: Can indicate reservations and reluctance, state conditions when agreeing to requests or granting permission, and ask for understanding of their own position.

429.en: Can engage in extended conversation on most general topics in a clearly participatory fashion, even in a [audially/visually] noisy environment.

430.en: Can sustain relationships with users of the target language without unintentionally amusing or irritating them or requiring them to behave other than they would with another proficient language user.

431.en: Can convey degrees of emotion and highlight the personal significance of events and experiences.

432.en: Can start up a conversation and help keep it going by asking people relatively spontaneous questions about a special experience or event, expressing reactions and opinions on familiar subjects.

433.en: Can have relatively long conversations on subjects of common interest, provided the interlocutor makes an effort to support understanding.

434.en: Can enter unprepared into conversations on familiar topics.

435.en: Can follow clearly articulated language directed at them in everyday conversation, though will sometimes have to ask for repetition of particular words/signs.

436.en: Can maintain a conversation or discussion but may sometimes be difficult to follow when trying to express exactly what they would like to.

437.en: Can express and respond to feelings such as surprise, happiness, sadness, interest and indifference.

438.en: Can establish social contact (e.g. greetings and farewells, introductions, giving thanks).

439.en: Can generally understand clear, standard language on familiar matters directed at them, provided they can ask for repetition or reformulation from time to time.

440.en: Can participate in short conversations in routine contexts on topics of interest.

441.en: Can express how they feel in simple terms, and express thanks.

442.en: Can ask for a favour (e.g. to borrow something), can offer a favour, and can respond if someone asks them to do a favour for them.

443.en: Can handle very short social exchanges but is rarely able to understand enough to keep conversation going of their own accord, though they can be made to understand if the interlocutor will take the trouble.

444.en: Can use simple, everyday, polite forms of greeting and address.

445.en: Can converse in simple language with peers, colleagues or members of a host family, asking questions and understanding answers relating to most routine matters.

446.en: Can make and respond to invitations, suggestions and apologies.

447.en: Can express how they are feeling, using very basic stock expressions.

448.en: Can state what they like and dislike.

449.en: Can understand everyday expressions aimed at the satisfaction of simple needs of a concrete type, delivered directly to them in clear, slow and repeated language by a sympathetic interlocutor.

450.en: Can take part in a simple conversation of a basic factual nature on a predictable topic (e.g. their home country, family, school).

451.en: Can make an introduction and use basic greeting and leave-taking expressions.

452.en: Can ask how people are and react to news.

453.en: Can understand and use basic, formulaic expressions such as “Yes”, “No”, “Excuse me”, “Please”, “Thank you”, “No thank you”, “Sorry”.

455.en: Can greet people, state their name and take leave in a simple way.

456.en: Can advise on or discuss sensitive issues without awkwardness, understanding colloquial references and dealing diplomatically with disagreement and criticism.

457.en: Can easily follow and contribute to complex interactions between third parties in group discussion even on abstract, complex unfamiliar topics.

458.en: Can keep up with an animated discussion between proficient users of the target language.

459.en: Can express their ideas and opinions with precision, and present and respond to complex lines of argument convincingly.

460.en: Can take an active part in informal discussion in familiar contexts, commenting, putting a point of view clearly, evaluating alternative proposals and making and responding to hypotheses.

461.en: Can with some effort catch much of what is said around them in discussion, but may find it difficult to participate effectively in discussion with several users of the target language who do not modify their language in any way.

462.en: Can account for and sustain their opinions in discussion by providing relevant explanations, arguments and comments.

463.en: Can follow much of what is said around them on general topics, provided interlocutors avoid very idiomatic usage and articulate clearly.

464.en: Can express their thoughts about abstract or cultural topics such as music or films.

465.en: Can explain why something is a problem.

466.en: Can give brief comments on the views of others.

467.en: Can compare and contrast alternatives, discussing what to do, where to go, who or which to choose, etc.

468.en: Can generally follow the main points in an informal discussion with friends provided they articulate clearly in standard language or a familiar variety.

469.en: Can give or seek personal views and opinions in discussing topics of interest.

470.en: Can make their opinions and reactions understood as regards solutions to problems or practical questions of where to go, what to do, or how to organise an event (e.g. an outing).

471.en: Can express beliefs, opinions and agreement and disagreement politely.

472.en: Can generally identify the topic of discussion around them when it is conducted slowly and clearly.

473.en: Can exchange opinions and compare things and people using simple language.

474.en: Can discuss what to do in the evening or at the weekend.

475.en: Can make and respond to suggestions.

476.en: Can agree and disagree with others.

477.en: Can discuss everyday practical issues in a simple way when addressed clearly, slowly and directly.

478.en: Can discuss what to do, where to go and make arrangements to meet.

479.en: Can express opinions in a limited way.

480.en: Can exchange likes and dislikes for sports, foods, etc., using a limited repertoire of expressions, when addressed clearly, slowly and directly.

482.en: Can hold their own in formal discussion of complex issues, putting an articulate and persuasive argument, at no disadvantage to other participants.

483.en: Can advise on/handle complex, delicate or contentious issues, provided they have the necessary specialised knowledge.

484.en: Can deal with hostile questioning confidently, hold on to the turn and diplomatically rebut counter-arguments.

485.en: Can easily keep up with the debate, even on abstract, complex, unfamiliar topics.

486.en: Can argue a formal position convincingly, responding to questions and comments and answering complex lines of counter-argument fluently, spontaneously and appropriately.

487.en: Can restate, evaluate and challenge contributions from other participants about matters within their academic or professional competence.

488.en: Can make critical remarks or express disagreement diplomatically.

489.en: Can follow up questions by probing for more detail and can reformulate questions if these are misunderstood.

490.en: Can keep up with an animated discussion, identifying accurately arguments supporting and opposing points of view.

491.en: Can use appropriate technical terminology when discussing their area of specialisation with other specialists.

492.en: Can express their ideas and opinions with precision, and present and respond to complex lines of argument convincingly.

493.en: Can participate actively in routine and non-routine formal discussion.

494.en: Can follow the discussion on matters related to their field, understand in detail the points given prominence.

495.en: Can contribute, account for and sustain their opinion, evaluate alternative proposals and make and respond to hypotheses.

496.en: Can follow much of what is said that is related to their field, provided interlocutors avoid very idiomatic usage and articulate clearly.

497.en: Can put over a point of view clearly, but has difficulty engaging in debate.

498.en: Can take part in routine formal discussion of familiar subjects which is clearly articulated in the standard form of the language or a familiar variety and which involves the exchange of factual information, receiving instructions or the discussion of solutions to practical problems.

499.en: Can follow argumentation and discussion on a familiar or predictable topic, provided the points are made in relatively simple language and/or repeated, and opportunity is given for clarification.

500.en: Can generally follow changes of topic in formal discussion related to their field which is conducted slowly and clearly.

501.en: Can exchange relevant information and give their opinion on practical problems when asked directly, provided they receive some help with formulation and can ask for repetition of key points if necessary.

502.en: Can express what they think about things when addressed directly in a formal meeting, provided they can ask for repetition of key points if necessary.

506.scale: Goal-oriented co-operation (cooking together, discussing a document, organising an event, etc.)
506.en: Can frame a discussion to decide on a course of action with a partner or group, reporting on what others have said, and summarising, elaborating and weighing up multiple points of view.

507.scale: Goal-oriented co-operation (cooking together, discussing a document, organising an event, etc.)

508.scale: Goal-oriented co-operation (cooking together, discussing a document, organising an event, etc.)
508.en: Can help along the progress of the work by inviting others to join in, express what they think, etc.

509.scale: Goal-oriented co-operation (cooking together, discussing a document, organising an event, etc.)
509.en: Can outline an issue or a problem clearly, speculating about causes or consequences, and weighing advantages and disadvantages of different approaches.

510.scale: Goal-oriented co-operation (cooking together, discussing a document, organising an event, etc.)
510.en: Can follow what is said, though they may occasionally have to ask for repetition or clarification if the discussion is rapid or extended.

511.scale: Goal-oriented co-operation (cooking together, discussing a document, organising an event, etc.)
511.en: Can explain why something is a problem, discuss what to do next, and compare and contrast alternatives.

512.scale: Goal-oriented co-operation (cooking together, discussing a document, organising an event, etc.)
512.en: Can give brief comments on the views of others.

513.scale: Goal-oriented co-operation (cooking together, discussing a document, organising an event, etc.)
513.en: Can generally follow what is said and, when necessary, repeat back part of what someone has said to confirm mutual understanding.

514.scale: Goal-oriented co-operation (cooking together, discussing a document, organising an event, etc.)
514.en: Can make their opinions and reactions understood as regards possible solutions or the question of what to do next, giving brief reasons and explanations.

515.scale: Goal-oriented co-operation (cooking together, discussing a document, organising an event, etc.)
515.en: Can invite others to give their views on how to proceed.

516.scale: Goal-oriented co-operation (cooking together, discussing a document, organising an event, etc.)
516.en: Can understand enough to manage simple, routine tasks without undue effort, asking very simply for repetition when they do not understand.

517.scale: Goal-oriented co-operation (cooking together, discussing a document, organising an event, etc.)
517.en: Can discuss what to do next, making and responding to suggestions, and asking for and giving directions.

518.scale: Goal-oriented co-operation (cooking together, discussing a document, organising an event, etc.)
518.en: Can indicate when they are following and can be made to understand what is necessary, if the interlocutor takes the trouble.

519.scale: Goal-oriented co-operation (cooking together, discussing a document, organising an event, etc.)
519.en: Can communicate in simple and routine tasks using simple phrases to ask for and provide things, to get simple information and to discuss what to do next.

520.scale: Goal-oriented co-operation (cooking together, discussing a document, organising an event, etc.)
520.en: Can understand questions and instructions addressed carefully and slowly to them and follow short, simple directions.

521.scale: Goal-oriented co-operation (cooking together, discussing a document, organising an event, etc.)
521.en: Can act on basic instructions that involve times, locations, numbers, etc.

522.scale: Goal-oriented co-operation (cooking together, discussing a document, organising an event, etc.)
522.en: Can ask people for things, and give people things.

525.en: Can negotiate complex or sensitive transactions in public, professional or academic life.

526.en: Can cope linguistically to negotiate a solution to a dispute like an undeserved traffic ticket, financial responsibility for damage in a flat, or blame regarding an accident.

527.en: Can outline a case for compensation, using persuasive language to demand satisfaction and state clearly the limits to any concession they are prepared to make.

528.en: Can state requirements and ask detailed questions regarding more complex services, e.g. rental agreements.

529.en: Can explain a problem which has arisen and make it clear that the provider of the service/customer must make a concession.

530.en: Can deal with most transactions likely to arise while travelling, arranging travel or accommodation, or dealing with authorities during a foreign visit.

531.en: Can ask in a shop for an explanation of the difference between two or more products serving the same purpose, in order to make a decision, posing follow-up questions as necessary.

532.en: Can cope with less routine situations in shops, post offices, banks, e.g. returning an unsatisfactory purchase.

534.en: Can deal with most situations likely to arise when making travel arrangements through an agent or when actually travelling, e.g. asking a passenger where to get off for an unfamiliar destination.

535.en: Can deal with common aspects of everyday living such as travel, lodging, eating and shopping.

536.en: Can interact in predictable everyday situations (e.g. post office, station, shop), using a wide range of simple expressions.

537.en: Can get all the information needed from a tourist office, as long as it is of a straightforward, non-specialised nature.

538.en: Can ask for and provide everyday goods and services.

539.en: Can get simple information about travel, use public transport (e.g. buses, trains, taxis), ask and give directions, and buy tickets.

540.en: Can ask about things and make simple transactions in shops, post offices or banks.

541.en: Can give and receive information about quantities, numbers, prices, etc.

542.en: Can make simple purchases by stating what is wanted and asking the price.

544.en: Can point out when something is wrong (e.g. “The food is cold” or “There is no light in my room”).

545.en: Can ask (face-to-face) for a medical appointment and understand the reply. Can indicate the nature of a problem to a health professional, perhaps using gestures and body language.

546.en: Can ask people for things and give people things.

547.en: Can ask for food and drink using basic expressions.

548.en: Can handle numbers, quantities, cost and time.

549.en: Can make simple purchases and/or order food or drink when pointing or other gesture can support the verbal reference.

552.en: Can understand and exchange complex information and advice on the full range of matters related to their occupational role.

553.en: Can use appropriate technical terminology when exchanging information or discussing their area of specialisation with other specialists.

554.en: Can pass on detailed information reliably.

555.en: Can exchange, check and confirm accumulated factual information on familiar routine and non-routine matters within their field with some confidence.

556.en: Can summarise and give their opinion about a short story, article, talk, discussion, interview or documentary and answer further questions of detail.

557.en: Can find out and pass on straightforward factual information.

558.en: Can ask for and follow detailed directions.

560.en: Can offer advice on simple matters within their field of experience.

561.en: Can understand enough to manage simple, routine exchanges without undue effort.

562.en: Can deal with practical everyday demands: finding out and passing on straightforward factual information.

563.en: Can ask and answer questions about habits and routines.

564.en: Can ask and answer questions about pastimes and past activities.

565.en: Can ask and answer questions about plans and intentions.

566.en: Can give and follow simple directions and instructions, e.g. explain how to get somewhere.

567.en: Can communicate in simple and routine tasks requiring a simple and direct exchange of information.

568.en: Can exchange limited information on familiar and routine operational matters.

569.en: Can ask and answer questions about what they do at work and in their free time.

570.en: Can ask for and give directions referring to a map or plan.

571.en: Can ask for and provide personal information.

572.en: Can ask and answer simple questions about an event (e.g. ask where and when it took place, who was there and what it was like).

573.en: Can understand questions and instructions addressed carefully and slowly to them and follow short, simple directions.

574.en: Can ask and answer simple questions, initiate and respond to simple statements in areas of immediate need or on very familiar topics.

575.en: Can ask and answer questions about themselves and other people, where they live, people they know, things they have.

576.en: Can indicate time by lexicalised phrases like “next week”, “last Friday”, “in November”, “three o’clock”.

577.en: Can express numbers, quantities and cost in a limited way.

578.en: Can name the colour of clothes or other familiar objects and can ask the colour of such objects.
