1057.en: Can make a set of instructions easier to understand by repeating them slowly, a few words/signs at a time, employing verbal and non-verbal emphasis to facilitate understanding.

1061.en: Can elucidate the information given in texts on complex academic or professional topics by elaborating and providing examples.

1062.en: Can make complex, challenging content more accessible by explaining difficult aspects more explicitly and adding helpful detail.

1063.en: Can make the main points contained in a complex text more accessible to the target audience by adding redundancy, explaining, and modifying style and register.

1064.en: Can make the content of a text on a subject in their fields of interest more accessible to a target audience by adding examples, reasoning and explanatory comments.

1065.en: Can make concepts on subjects in their fields of interest more accessible by giving concrete examples, recapitulating step by step and repeating the main points.

1066.en: Can make new information more accessible by using repetition and adding illustrations.

1067.en: Can make an aspect of an everyday topic clearer and more explicit by conveying the main information in another way.

1068.en: Can make an aspect of an everyday topic clearer by providing simple examples.

1072.en: Can redraft a complex source text, improving coherence, cohesion and the flow of an argument, while removing sections unnecessary for its purpose.

1073.en: Can reorganise a complex source text in order to focus on the points of most relevance to the target audience.

1074.en: Can simplify a source text by excluding non-relevant or repetitive information and taking into consideration the intended audience.

1075.en: Can edit a source text by deleting the parts that do not add new information that is relevant for a given audience in order to make the significant content more accessible for them.

1076.en: Can identify related or repeated information in different parts of a text and merge it in order to make the essential message clearer.

1077.en: Can identify and mark (e.g. underline, highlight) the essential information in a straightforward, informational text, in order to pass this information on to someone else.

1078.en: Can identify and mark (e.g. underline, highlight) the key sentences in a short everyday text.

1081.en: Can initiate and control their actions and forms of expression according to context, showing awareness of cultural differences and making subtle adjustments in order to prevent and/or repair misunderstandings and cultural incidents.

1082.en: Can identify differences in sociolinguistic/-pragmatic conventions, critically reflect on them and adjust their communication accordingly.

1083.en: Can sensitively explain the background to and interpret and discuss aspects of cultural values and practices drawing on intercultural encounters, reading, film, etc.

1084.en: Can deal with ambiguity in cross-cultural communication and express their reactions constructively and culturally appropriately in order to bring clarity.

1085.en: **Can describe and evaluate the viewpoints and practices of their own and other social groups, showing awareness of the implicit values on which judgments and prejudices are frequently based.

1086.en: **Can explain their interpretation of the cultural assumptions, preconceptions, stereotypes and prejudices of their own community and of other communities that they are familiar with.

1087.en: **Can interpret and explain a document or event from another culture and relate it to documents or events from their own culture(s) and/or from cultures with which they are familiar.

1088.en: **Can discuss the objectivity and balance of information and opinions expressed in the media about their own and other communities.

1089.en: Can identify and reflect on similarities and differences in culturally determined behavioural patterns (e.g. gestures and speech volume or, for sign languages, sign size) and discuss their significance in order to negotiate mutual understanding.

1090.en: Can, in an intercultural encounter, recognise that what one normally takes for granted in a particular situation is not necessarily shared by others, and can react and express themselves appropriately.

1091.en: Can generally interpret cultural cues appropriately in the culture concerned.

1092.en: Can reflect on and explain particular ways of communicating in their own and other cultures, and the risks of misunderstanding they generate.

1093.en: Can generally act according to conventions regarding posture, eye contact and distance from others.

1094.en: Can generally respond appropriately to the most commonly used cultural cues.

1095.en: Can explain features of their own culture to members of another culture or explain features of the other culture to members of their own culture.

1096.en: Can explain in simple terms how their own values and behaviours influence their views of other people’s values and behaviours.

1097.en: Can discuss in simple terms the way in which things that may look “strange” to them in another sociocultural context may well be “normal” for the other people concerned.

1098.en: Can discuss in simple terms the way their own culturally determined actions may be perceived differently by people from other cultures.

1099.en: Can recognise and apply basic cultural conventions associated with everyday social exchanges (e.g. different greetings, rituals).

1100.en: Can act appropriately in everyday greetings, farewells and expressions of thanks and apology, although they have difficulty coping with any departure from the routine.

1101.en: Can recognise that their behaviour in an everyday transaction may convey a message different from the one they intend, and can try to explain this simply.

1102.en: Can recognise when difficulties occur in interaction with members of other cultures, even though they may not be sure how to behave in the situation.

1103.en: Can recognise differing ways of numbering, measuring distance, telling the time, etc. even though they may have difficulty applying this in even simple everyday transactions of a concrete type.

1107.en: Can use their knowledge of contrasting genre conventions and textual patterns in languages in their plurilingual repertoire in order to support comprehension.

1108.en: Can use what they have understood in one language to understand the topic and main message of a text in another language (e.g. when reading short newspaper articles in different languages on the same theme).

1109.en: Can use parallel translations of texts (e.g. magazine articles, stories, passages from novels) to develop comprehension in different languages.

1110.en: Can deduce the message of a text by exploiting what they have understood from texts on the same theme in different languages (e.g. news in brief, museum brochures, online reviews).

1111.en: Can extract information from documents in different languages in their field (e.g. to include in a presentation).

1112.en: Can recognise similarities and contrasts between the way concepts are expressed in different languages, in order to distinguish between identical uses of the same word/sign and “false friends”.

1113.en: Can use their knowledge of contrasting grammatical structures and functional expressions of languages in their plurilingual repertoire in order to support comprehension.

1114.en: Can understand short, clearly articulated announcements by piecing together what they understand from the available versions in different languages.

1115.en: Can understand short, clearly expressed messages and instructions by piecing together what they understand from the versions in different languages.

1116.en: Can use simple warnings, instructions and product information given in parallel in different languages to find relevant information.

1117.en: Can recognise internationalisms and words/signs common to different languages (e.g. haus/hus/house) to: deduce the meaning of simple signs and notices; identify the probable message of a short, simple text; follow in outline short, simple social exchanges conducted very slowly and clearly in their presence; deduce what people are trying to say directly to them, provided the articulation is very slow and clear, with repetition if necessary.

1119.en: Can interact in a multilingual context on abstract and specialised topics by alternating flexibly between languages in their plurilingual repertoire and if necessary explaining the different contributions made.

1120.en: Can explore similarities and differences between metaphors and other figures of speech in the languages in their plurilingual repertoire, either for rhetorical effect or for fun.

1121.en: Can alternate between languages flexibly to facilitate communication in a multilingual context, summarising and glossing in different languages in their plurilingual repertoire contributions to the discussion and texts referred to.

1122.en: Can participate effectively in a conversation in two or more languages in their plurilingual repertoire, adjusting to the changes of language and catering to the needs and linguistic skills of the interlocutors.

1123.en: Can use and explain specialised terminology from another language in their plurilingual repertoire more familiar to the interlocutor(s), in order to improve understanding in a discussion of abstract and specialised topics.

1124.en: Can respond spontaneously and flexibly in the appropriate language when someone else changes to another language in their plurilingual repertoire.

1125.en: Can support comprehension and discussion of a text spoken, signed or written in one language by explaining, summarising, clarifying and expanding it in another language in their plurilingual repertoire.

1126.en: **Can recognise the extent to which it is appropriate to make flexible use of different languages in their plurilingual repertoire in a specific situation, in order to increase the efficiency of communication.

1127.en: **Can alternate efficiently between languages in their plurilingual repertoire in order to facilitate comprehension with and between third parties who lack a common language.

1128.en: **Can introduce into an utterance an expression from another language in their plurilingual repertoire that is particularly apt for the situation/concept being discussed, explaining it for the interlocutor when necessary.

1129.en: Can alternate between languages in their plurilingual repertoire in order to communicate specialised information and issues on a subject in their field of interest to different interlocutors.

1130.en: Can make use of different languages in their plurilingual repertoire during collaborative interaction, in order to clarify the nature of a task, the main steps, the decisions to be taken and the outcomes expected.

1131.en: Can make use of different languages in their plurilingual repertoire to encourage other people to use the language in which they feel more comfortable

1132.en: Can exploit creatively their limited repertoire in different languages in their plurilingual repertoire for everyday contexts, in order to cope with an unexpected situation.

1133.en: Can mobilise their limited repertoire in different languages in order to explain a problem or to ask for help or clarification.

1134.en: Can use simple words/signs and phrases from different languages in their plurilingual repertoire to conduct a simple, practical transaction or information exchange.

1135.en: Can use a simple word/sign from another language in their plurilingual repertoire to make themselves understood in a routine everyday situation, when they cannot think of an adequate expression in the language being used.

1136.en: Can use a very limited repertoire in different languages to conduct a very basic, concrete, everyday transaction with a collaborative interlocutor.

1138.en: Can exploit a comprehensive and reliable mastery of a very wide range of language to formulate thoughts precisely, give emphasis, differentiate and eliminate ambiguity. No signs of having to restrict what they want to say.

1139.en: Can use a broad range of complex grammatical structures appropriately and with considerable flexibility.

1140.en: Can select an appropriate formulation from a broad range of language to express themselves clearly, without having to restrict what they want to say.

1141.en: Can express themselves clearly without much sign of having to restrict what they want to say.

1142.en: Has a sufficient range of language to be able to give clear descriptions, express viewpoints and develop arguments without much conspicuous searching for words/signs, using some complex sentence forms to do so.

1143.en: Has a sufficient range of language to describe unpredictable situations, explain the main points in an idea or problem with reasonable precision and express thoughts on abstract or cultural topics such as music and film.

1144.en: Has enough language to get by, with sufficient vocabulary to express themselves with some hesitation and circumlocutions on topics such as family, hobbies and interests, work, travel and current events, but lexical limitations cause repetition and even difficulty with formulation at times.

1145.en: Has a repertoire of basic language which enables them to deal with everyday situations with predictable content, though they will generally have to compromise the message and search for words/signs.

1146.en: Can produce brief, everyday expressions in order to satisfy simple needs of a concrete type (e.g. personal details, daily routines, wants and needs, requests for information).

1147.en: Can use basic sentence patterns and communicate with memorised phrases, groups of a few words/signs and formulae about themselves and other people, what they do, places, possessions, etc.

1148.en: Has a limited repertoire of short, memorised phrases covering predictable survival situations; frequent breakdowns and misunderstandings occur in non-routine situations.

1149.en: Has a very basic range of simple expressions about personal details and needs of a concrete type.

1150.en: Can use some basic structures in one-clause sentences with some omission or reduction of elements.

1151.en: Can use isolated words/signs and basic expressions in order to give simple information about themselves.

1152.en: Has a good command of a very broad lexical repertoire including idiomatic expressions and colloquialisms; shows awareness of connotative levels of meaning.

1153.en: Has a good command of a broad lexical repertoire allowing gaps to be readily overcome with circumlocutions; little obvious searching for expressions or avoidance strategies.

1154.en: Can select from several vocabulary options in almost all situations by exploiting synonyms of even words/signs less commonly encountered.

1155.en: Has a good command of common idiomatic expressions and colloquialisms; can play with words/signs fairly well.

1156.en: Can understand and use appropriately the range of technical vocabulary and idiomatic expressions common to their area of specialisation.

1157.en: Can understand and use the main technical terminology of their field, when discussing their area of specialisation with other specialists.

1158.en: Has a good range of vocabulary for matters connected to their field and most general topics.

1159.en: Can vary formulation to avoid frequent repetition, but lexical gaps can still cause hesitation and circumlocution.

1160.en: Can produce appropriate collocations of many words/signs in most contexts fairly systematically.

1161.en: Can understand and use much of the specialist vocabulary of their field but has problems with specialist terminology outside it.

1162.en: Has a good range of vocabulary related to familiar topics and everyday situations.

1163.en: Has sufficient vocabulary to express themselves with some circumlocutions on most topics pertinent to their everyday life such as family, hobbies and interests, work, travel and current events.

1164.en: Has sufficient vocabulary to conduct routine everyday transactions involving familiar situations and topics.

1165.en: Has sufficient vocabulary for the expression of basic communicative needs.

1166.en: Has sufficient vocabulary for coping with simple survival needs.

1167.en: Has a basic vocabulary repertoire of words/signs and phrases related to particular concrete situations.

1169.en: Maintains consistent grammatical control of complex language, even while attention is otherwise engaged (e.g. in forward planning, in monitoring others’ reactions).

1170.en: Consistently maintains a high degree of grammatical accuracy; errors are rare and difficult to spot.

1171.en: Good grammatical control; occasional “slips” or non-systematic errors and minor flaws in sentence structure may still occur, but they are rare and can often be corrected in retrospect.

1172.en: Shows a relatively high degree of grammatical control. Does not make mistakes which lead to misunderstanding.

1173.en: Has a good command of simple language structures and some complex grammatical forms, although they tend to use complex structures rigidly with some inaccuracy.

1174.en: Communicates with reasonable accuracy in familiar contexts; generally good control, though with noticeable mother-tongue influence. Errors occur, but it is clear what they are trying to express.

1175.en: Uses reasonably accurately a repertoire of frequently used “routines” and patterns associated with more predictable situations.

1176.en: Uses some simple structures correctly, but still systematically makes basic mistakes; nevertheless, it is usually clear what they are trying to say.

1177.en: Shows only limited control of a few simple grammatical structures and sentence patterns in a learnt repertoire.

1178.en: Can employ very simple principles of word/sign order in short statements.

1179.en: Consistently correct and appropriate use of vocabulary.

1180.en: Uses less common vocabulary idiomatically and appropriately.

1181.en: Occasional minor slips, but no significant vocabulary errors.

1182.en: Lexical accuracy is generally high, though some confusion and incorrect word/sign choice does occur without hindering communication.

1183.en: Shows good control of elementary vocabulary but major errors still occur when expressing more complex thoughts or handling unfamiliar topics and situations.

1184.en: Uses a wide range of simple vocabulary appropriately when discussing familiar topics.

1185.en: Can control a narrow repertoire dealing with concrete, everyday needs.

1188.en: Can employ the full range of phonological features in the target language with a high level of control – including prosodic features such as word and sentence stress, rhythm and intonation – so that the finer points of their message are clear and precise. Intelligibility and effective conveyance and enhancement of meaning are not affected in any way by features of accent that may be retained from other language(s).

1189.en: Can employ the full range of phonological features in the target language with sufficient control to ensure intelligibility throughout. Can articulate virtually all the sounds of the target language; some features of accent(s) retained from other language(s) may be noticeable, but they do not affect intelligibility.

1190.en: Can generally use appropriate intonation, place stress correctly and articulate individual sounds clearly; accent tends to be influenced by the other language(s) they speak, but has little or no effect on intelligibility.

1191.en: Pronunciation is generally intelligible; intonation and stress at both utterance and word levels do not prevent understanding of the message. Accent is usually influenced by the other language(s) they speak.

1192.en: Pronunciation is generally clear enough to be understood, but conversational partners will need to ask for repetition from time to time. A strong influence from the other language(s) they speak on stress, rhythm and intonation may affect intelligibility, requiring collaboration from interlocutors. Nevertheless, pronunciation of familiar words is clear.

1193.en: Pronunciation of a very limited repertoire of learnt words and phrases can be understood with some effort by interlocutors used to dealing with speakers of the language group. Can reproduce correctly a limited range of sounds as well as stress for simple, familiar words and phrases.

1194.en: Can articulate virtually all the sounds of the target language with clarity and precision.

1195.en: Can articulate virtually all the sounds of the target language with a high degree of control. They can usually self-correct if they noticeably mispronounce a sound.

1196.en: Can articulate a high proportion of the sounds in the target language clearly in extended stretches of production; is intelligible throughout, despite a few systematic mispronunciations.

1197.en: Can generalise from their repertoire to predict the phonological features of most unfamiliar words (e.g. word stress) with reasonable accuracy (e.g. while reading).

1198.en: Is generally intelligible throughout, despite regular mispronunciation of individual sounds and words they are less familiar with.

1199.en: Pronunciation is generally intelligible when communicating in simple everyday situations, provided the interlocutor makes an effort to understand specific sounds.

1200.en: Systematic mispronunciation of phonemes does not hinder intelligibility, provided the interlocutor makes an effort to recognise and adjust to the influence of the speaker’s language background on pronunciation.

1201.en: Can reproduce sounds in the target language if carefully guided.

1202.en: Can articulate a limited number of sounds, so that speech is only intelligible if the interlocutor provides support (e.g. by repeating correctly and by eliciting repetition of new sounds).

1203.en: Can exploit prosodic features (e.g. stress, rhythm and intonation) appropriately and effectively in order to convey finer shades of meaning (e.g. to differentiate and emphasise).

1204.en: Can produce smooth, intelligible spoken discourse with only occasional lapses in control of stress, rhythm and/or intonation, which do not affect intelligibility or effectiveness.

1205.en: Can vary intonation and place stress correctly in order to express precisely what they mean to say.

1206.en: Can employ prosodic features (e.g. stress, intonation, rhythm) to support the message they intend to convey, though with some influence from the other languages they speak.

1207.en: Can convey their message in an intelligible way in spite of a strong influence on stress, intonation and/or rhythm from the other language(s) they speak.

1208.en: Can use the prosodic features of everyday words and phrases intelligibly, in spite of a strong influence on stress, intonation and/or rhythm from the other language(s) they speak.

1209.en: Prosodic features (e.g. word stress) are adequate for familiar everyday words and simple utterances.

1210.en: Can use the prosodic features of a limited repertoire of simple words and phrases intelligibly, in spite of a very strong influence on stress, rhythm and/or intonation from the other language(s) they speak; their interlocutor needs to be collaborative.

1211.en: Writing is orthographically free of error.

1212.en: Layout, paragraphing and punctuation are consistent and helpful.

1213.en: Spelling is accurate, apart from occasional slips of the pen.

1214.en: Can produce clearly intelligible, continuous writing which follows standard layout and paragraphing conventions.

1215.en: Spelling and punctuation are reasonably accurate but may show signs of mother-tongue influence.

1216.en: Can produce continuous writing which is generally intelligible throughout.

1217.en: Spelling, punctuation and layout are accurate enough to be followed most of the time.

1218.en: Can copy short sentences on everyday subjects, e.g. directions on how to get somewhere.

1219.en: Can write with reasonable phonetic accuracy (but not necessarily fully standard spelling) short words that are in their oral vocabulary.

1220.en: Can copy familiar words and short phrases, e.g. simple signs or instructions, names of everyday objects, names of shops, and set phrases used regularly.

1221.en: Can spell their address, nationality and other personal details.

1222.en: Can use basic punctuation (e.g. full stops, question marks).

1224.en: Can mediate effectively and naturally between users of the target language and members of their own community, taking account of sociocultural and sociolinguistic differences.

1225.en: Has a good command of idiomatic expressions and colloquialisms with awareness of connotative levels of meaning.

1226.en: Appreciates virtually all the sociolinguistic and sociocultural implications of language used by proficient users of the target language and can react accordingly.

1227.en: Can effectively employ, both orally and in writing, a wide variety of sophisticated language to command, argue, persuade, dissuade, negotiate and counsel.

1228.en: Can recognise a wide range of idiomatic expressions and colloquialisms, appreciating register shifts; may, however, need to confirm occasional details, especially if the accent is unfamiliar.

1229.en: Can understand humour, irony and implicit cultural references and pick up nuances of meaning.

1230.en: Can follow films employing a considerable degree of slang and idiomatic usage.

1231.en: Can use language flexibly and effectively for social purposes, including emotional, allusive and joking usage.

1232.en: Can adjust their level of formality (register and style) to suit the social context: formal, informal or colloquial as appropriate, and maintain a consistent register.

1233.en: Can frame critical remarks or express strong disagreement diplomatically.

1234.en: Can with some effort keep up with and contribute to group discussions even when talk is fast and colloquial.

1235.en: Can recognise and interpret sociocultural/sociolinguistic cues and consciously modify their linguistic forms of expression in order to express themselves appropriately in the situation.

1236.en: Can express themselves confidently, clearly and politely in a formal or informal register, appropriate to the situation and person(s) concerned.

1237.en: Can adjust their expression to make some distinction between formal and informal registers but may not always do so appropriately.

1238.en: Can sustain relationships with users of the target language without unintentionally amusing or irritating them or requiring them to behave other than they would with another proficient user.

1239.en: Can express themselves appropriately in situations and avoid crass errors of formulation.

1240.en: Can perform and respond to a wide range of language functions, using their most common exponents in a neutral register.

1241.en: Is aware of the salient politeness conventions and acts appropriately.

1242.en: Is aware of, and looks out for signs of, the most significant differences between the customs, usages, attitudes, values and beliefs prevalent in the community concerned and those of their own community.

1243.en: Can perform and respond to basic language functions, e.g. information exchange and requests, and express opinions and attitudes in a simple way.

1244.en: Can socialise simply but effectively using the simplest common expressions and following basic routines.

1245.en: Can handle very short social exchanges, using everyday polite forms of greeting and address.

1246.en: Can make and respond to invitations, suggestions, apologies, etc.

1247.en: Can establish basic social contact by using the simplest everyday polite forms of: greetings and farewells; introductions; saying please, thank you, sorry, etc.

1249.en: Shows great flexibility in reformulating ideas in differing linguistic forms to give emphasis, differentiate according to the situation, interlocutor, etc. and to eliminate ambiguity.

1250.en: Can make a positive impact on an intended audience by effectively varying style of expression and sentence length, use of advanced vocabulary and word order.

1251.en: Can modify their expression to express degrees of commitment or hesitation, confidence or uncertainty.

1252.en: Can adjust what they say and the means of expressing it to the situation and the recipient and adopt a level of formality appropriate to the circumstances.

1253.en: Can adjust to the changes of direction, style and emphasis normally found in conversation.

1254.en: Can vary formulation of what they want to say.

1255.en: Can reformulate an idea to emphasise or explain a point.

1256.en: Can adapt their expression to deal with less routine, even difficult, situations.

1257.en: Can exploit a wide range of simple language flexibly to express much of what they want.

1258.en: Can adapt well-rehearsed, memorised, simple phrases to particular circumstances through limited lexical substitution.

1259.en: Can expand learnt phrases through simple recombinations of their elements.

1263.en: Can select a suitable phrase from a readily available range of discourse functions to preface their remarks appropriately in order to get the floor, or to gain time and keep the floor whilst thinking.

1264.en: Can intervene appropriately in discussion, exploiting appropriate language to do so.

1265.en: Can initiate, maintain and end discourse appropriately with effective turntaking.

1266.en: Can initiate discourse, take their turn when appropriate and end conversation when they need to, though they may not always do this elegantly.

1267.en: Can use stock phrases (e.g. “That’s a difficult question to answer”) to gain time and keep the turn whilst formulating what they want to express.

1268.en: Can intervene in a discussion on a familiar topic, using a suitable phrase to get the floor.

1269.en: Can initiate, maintain and close simple face-to-face conversation on topics that are familiar or of personal interest.

1270.en: Can use simple techniques to start, maintain or close a short conversation.

1271.en: Can initiate, maintain and close simple, face-to-face conversation.

1275.en: Can use the conventions of the type of text concerned with sufficient flexibility to communicate complex ideas in an effective way, holding the target reader’s attention with ease and fulfilling all communicative purposes.

1276.en: Can use the conventions of the type of text concerned to hold the target reader’s attention and communicate complex ideas.

1277.en: Can give elaborate descriptions and narratives, integrating sub-themes, developing particular points and rounding off with an appropriate conclusion.

1278.en: Can write a suitable introduction and conclusion to a long, complex text.

1279.en: Can expand and support the main points at some length with subsidiary points, reasons and relevant examples.

1280.en: Can develop an argument systematically with appropriate highlighting of significant points, and relevant supporting detail.

1281.en: Can present and respond to complex lines of argument convincingly.

1282.en: Can follow the conventional structure of the communicative task concerned when communicating their ideas.

1283.en: Can develop a clear description or narrative, expanding and supporting their main points with relevant supporting detail and examples.

1284.en: Can develop a clear argument, expanding and supporting their points of view at some length with subsidiary points and relevant examples.

1285.en: Can evaluate the advantages and disadvantages of various options.

1286.en: Can clearly signal the difference between fact and opinion.

1287.en: Can clearly signal chronological sequence in narrative text.

1288.en: Can develop an argument well enough to be followed without difficulty most of the time.

1289.en: Shows awareness of the conventional structure of the text type concerned when communicating their ideas.

1290.en: Can reasonably fluently relate a straightforward narrative or description as a sequence of points.

1291.en: Can tell a story or describe something in a simple list of points.

1292.en: Can give an example of something in a very simple text using “like” or “for example”.

1296.en: Can create coherent and cohesive text making full and appropriate use of a variety of organisational patterns and a wide range of cohesive devices.

1297.en: Can produce clear, smoothly flowing, well-structured language, showing controlled use of organisational patterns, connectors and cohesive devices.

1298.en: Can produce well-organised, coherent text, using a variety of cohesive devices and organisational patterns.

1299.en: Can use a variety of linking expressions efficiently to mark clearly the relationships between ideas.

1300.en: Can use a limited number of cohesive devices to link their utterances into clear, coherent discourse, though there may be some “jumpiness” in a long contribution.

1301.en: Can produce text that is generally well-organised and coherent, using a range of linking expressions and cohesive devices.

1302.en: Can structure longer texts in clear, logical paragraphs.

1303.en: Can introduce a counter-argument in a simple discursive text (e.g. with “however”).

1304.en: Can link a series of shorter, discrete simple elements into a connected, linear sequence of points.

1305.en: Can form longer sentences and link them together using a limited number of cohesive devices, e.g. in a story.

1306.en: Can make simple, logical paragraph breaks in a longer text.

1307.en: Can use the most frequently occurring connectors to link simple sentences in order to tell a story or describe something as a simple list of points.

1308.en: Can link groups of words/signs with simple connectors (e.g. “and”, “but” and “because”).

1309.en: Can link words/signs or groups of words/signs with very basic linear connectors (e.g. “and” or “then”).

1311.en: Can convey finer shades of meaning precisely by using, with reasonable accuracy, a wide range of qualifying devices (e.g. adverbs expressing degree, clauses expressing limitations).

1312.en: Can give emphasis, differentiate and eliminate ambiguity.

1313.en: Can qualify opinions and statements precisely in relation to degrees of, for example, certainty/uncertainty, belief/doubt, likelihood, etc.

1314.en: Can make effective use of linguistic modality to signal the strength of a claim, an argument or a position.

1315.en: Can pass on detailed information reliably.

1316.en: Can communicate the essential points even in more demanding situations, though their language lacks expressive power and idiomaticity.

1317.en: Can explain the main points in an idea or problem with reasonable precision.

1318.en: Can convey simple, straightforward information of immediate relevance, getting across the point they feel is most important.

1319.en: Can express the main point they want to make comprehensibly.

1320.en: Can communicate what they want to say in a simple and direct exchange of limited information on familiar and routine matters, but in other situations they generally have to compromise the message.

1321.en: Can communicate basic information about personal details and needs of a concrete type in a simple way.

1322.en: Can communicate very basic information about personal details in a simple way.

1323.en: Can express themselves at length with a natural, effortless, unhesitating flow. Pauses only to reflect on precisely the right means to express their thoughts or to find an appropriate example or explanation.

1324.en: Can express themselves fluently and spontaneously, almost effortlessly. Only a conceptually difficult subject can hinder a natural, smooth flow of language.

1325.en: Can communicate spontaneously, often showing remarkable fluency and ease of expression in even longer complex stretches of language.

1326.en: Can produce stretches of language with a fairly even tempo; although they can be hesitant as they search for patterns and expressions, there are few noticeably long pauses.

1327.en: Can interact with a degree of fluency and spontaneity that makes regular interaction with users of the target language quite possible without imposing strain on either party.

1328.en: Can express themselves with relative ease. Despite some problems with formulation resulting in pauses and “cul-de-sacs”, they are able to keep going effectively without help.

1329.en: Can keep going comprehensibly, even though pausing for grammatical and lexical planning and repair is very evident, especially in longer stretches of free production.

1330.en: Can make themselves understood in short contributions, even though pauses, false starts and reformulation are very evident.

1331.en: Can construct phrases on familiar topics with sufficient ease to handle short exchanges, despite very noticeable hesitation and false starts.

1332.en: Can manage very short, isolated, mainly pre-packaged utterances, with much pausing to search for expressions, to articulate less familiar words/signs, and to repair communication.

1333.en: Can manage very short, isolated, rehearsed utterances using gesture and signalled requests for help when necessary.
